content.text: Dynamic components extend generative UI by letting the model construct a nested component tree — not just select a single widget. The model can output a

, all specified as a JSON tree. The renderer traverses that tree and mounts each named component from a static allowlist. The key constraint: the model cannot introduce new component types, inject functions, or generate arbitrary HTML — it can only assemble from the components the developer has registered.

attrs.alt: Dynamic component tree: model outputs a JSON ComponentSpec tree → registry resolves names to React.lazy components → renderer mounts each node with error boundaries

content.text: Every component the model may request is registered in advance with
. The registry is the complete allowlist — any name not in it is rejected at render time, not validated against a separate schema.
object: a type name, an optional props map, and optional children (themselves
nodes). Validate the full tree with a recursive Zod schema before rendering. The tool schema sent to the model should list the allowed type names as an enum.

The renderer validates the spec, enforces a depth limit, looks up the component in the registry, and wraps each node in an error boundary and a Suspense boundary. Unknown names produce a visible fallback rather than throwing.
MAX_DEPTH = 3 prevents infinite recursion from a model that generates deep trees
ComponentErrorBoundary catches runtime errors in individual components — one broken child cannot crash the whole dashboard
Suspense with a skeleton handles the async import() from React.lazy
validateComponentSpec is called before registry lookup — a ZodError renders a JSON dump, not an application crash
A top-level Zod schema for each component prevents the model from injecting unexpected prop values. Restrict prop types to primitives, arrays of primitives, and safe enum values. Never accept

The tool schema sent to the model lists allowed component names as an enum in the input_schema. The model fills in the tree; the server returns it as-is to the frontend, which validates and renders it.

# agent.py — generate a ComponentSpec tree from a user request
import anthropic, json
from component_schema import RENDER_DASHBOARD_TOOL

client = anthropic.AsyncAnthropic()

SYSTEM = """You are a data dashboard assistant. When the user asks to visualise data,
call render_dashboard with a component tree. Use DashboardLayout as the root for
multi-panel layouts. Nest MetricCard, LineChart, and DataTable as children.
Keep trees to at most 8 components total."""

async def get_dashboard(user_request: str, data: dict) -> dict | None:
    """Return a ComponentSpec dict from the model, or None if it chose prose."""
    response = await client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-8",
        max_tokens=2048,
        system=SYSTEM,
        messages=[{"role":"user","content":f"Request: {user_request}\nData: {json.dumps(data)}"}],
        tools=[RENDER_DASHBOARD_TOOL],
    )
    for block in response.content:
        if block.type == "tool_use" and block.name == "render_dashboard":
            return block.input.get("layout")
    return None
Prevents loading arbitrary modules or executing code
Props from a language model are untrusted data
Functions cannot be serialised in JSON — any function in props is developer code
Prevents O(n^k) rendering cost from deeply nested trees
No onClick / onSubmit from model output
Event handlers must be developer-defined, not model-generated strings

Dynamic components let a model assemble a UI from a predefined component library without the developer hard-coding which layout to show. The safety model is strict: maintain a static registry, validate the component tree with Zod before rendering, enforce a depth limit, wrap every node in an error boundary, and never accept event handlers or HTML from the model. The developer controls which components exist and what their props look like; the model controls how they are assembled for a given request. This keeps the pattern safe to deploy while still giving the model meaningful control over the UI shape.
